style_notes.easy_questions: definition-focused vocabulary checks with less application
style_notes.hard_questions: relevant distractors with minimal recycling, including partially correct College Board-style traps
style_notes.analysis_questions: clear, concrete experimental scenarios with understandable setups and interpretable data
style_notes.answer_keys: specific explanations for incorrect choices that identify the exact misconception or why the option is tempting but wrong
style_notes.carryover_policy: older valid question patterns may be kept if they still meet the updated quality standards

questions.text:
A population of deer is being studied over several years.
Which change would best represent a density-dependent limit on the population?
choices: A random weather event occurs, because all environmental factors become density-dependent once they affect a living population.
The population experiences little change in survival, because density-dependent factors matter only after a population has already reached zero growth.
A severe storm strikes the habitat, because any event that lowers population size is automatically density-dependent.
a fungal disease spreads much faster once individuals begin nesting close together, because disease transmission increases when organisms are crowded.
questions.explain: A density-dependent factor is one whose effect becomes stronger as population density increases, such as competition, disease, or parasitism.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because environmental factors do affect populations, but it is wrong because density-dependent factors are defined by dependence on population density, not simply by being part of the environment.

This choice is wrong because density-dependent factors can operate well before zero growth is reached. They commonly influence survival or reproduction whenever crowding increases competition, disease, or other interactions.

This choice is wrong because reducing population size does not automatically make a factor density-dependent. Storms are typically density-independent because they affect populations regardless of crowding.
Correct. This is density-dependent because disease transmission increases when organisms are crowded, so its effect becomes stronger as population density increases.

questions.text:
Researchers graph the size of a population of bacteria in a closed flask over time.
Which observation best supports logistic rather than exponential growth?
choices: The population shows no relationship to resource availability because carrying capacity is a feature of exponential growth only.
The population drops to zero immediately after one disturbance because any decline means logistic growth has ended.
The population doubles at a constant per capita rate indefinitely because density-dependent limits disappear once growth begins.
The population rises quickly at first and then levels off near a stable size because resources become limiting as density increases.
questions.explain: Logistic growth is characterized by an initial rapid increase followed by slowing growth as the population approaches carrying capacity.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because carrying capacity is central to logistic growth, not exponential growth.

This choice is partially correct because populations can decline during logistic dynamics, but it is wrong because a sudden crash alone does not define logistic growth. The hallmark is slowing growth near carrying capacity.
This choice is wrong because indefinite constant per capita growth describes exponential growth, not logistic growth. Logistic growth slows as limiting factors intensify.
Correct. Logistic growth shows rapid early growth followed by slowing and leveling near carrying capacity as density-dependent limits strengthen.

questions.text: Which observation would most strongly indicate exponential growth in an invasive insect newly introduced to a forest with abundant host plants?
choices: The population size increases by a constant proportion per unit time while resources are still effectively unlimited.
The population rises and then stabilizes around a maximum sustainable size because exponential growth always ends at carrying capacity.
The population decreases whenever individuals compete with one another because exponential growth requires zero births and zero deaths.
The population remains constant through time because exponential growth means the exponent stays the same, not the population size.
questions.explain: Exponential growth occurs when a population increases at a constant per capita rate under conditions of abundant resources and little immediate limitation.
choice_explanations: Correct. Exponential growth occurs when per capita growth remains high because limiting resources have not yet constrained the population.
This choice is partially correct because many real populations eventually stop growing, but leveling at a maximum sustainable size is logistic growth, not the defining pattern of exponential growth.
This choice is wrong because exponential growth does not require zero births and deaths. It requires births to exceed deaths at a roughly constant per capita rate.
This choice is wrong because exponential growth refers to accelerating increase in population size, not a constant population size.

questions.text: Which statement best matches the survivorship pattern expected for humans in a developed country?
choices: They are expected to show type III survivorship because any species with juveniles automatically has the highest mortality late in life.
They are expected to show type I survivorship because all organisms with many offspring keep mortality low until late life.
They are expected to show a type I survivorship curve because most individuals survive to old age and mortality rises steeply late in life.
They are expected to show type II survivorship because survivorship curves describe population growth rate rather than age-specific mortality.
questions.explain: Survivorship curves compare patterns of age-specific mortality. Type I has low early mortality, type II has fairly constant mortality, and type III has high early mortality.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because all species have juveniles, but it is wrong because type III refers to high early mortality, not mortality concentrated late in life.
This choice is wrong because producing many offspring is more often associated with high early mortality, not the low early mortality pattern of type I survivorship.
Correct. Humans in a developed country fit a type I survivorship pattern because most individuals survive to old age and mortality rises steeply late in life.
This choice is wrong because survivorship curves track age-specific survival, not overall population growth rate.

questions.text:
A population of deer overshoots the carrying capacity of its environment after several seasons of rapid growth.
Which result is most likely next if resources remain limited?
choices: The carrying capacity must rise immediately because any increase in population size forces the environment to support more individuals.
Birth rates may fall and death rates may rise until population size moves back toward carrying capacity.
The population will always crash to extinction because overshoot can never be followed by stabilization.
The population will continue growing exponentially because carrying capacity limits only individual body size, not population size.
questions.explain: Carrying capacity is the maximum population size the environment can sustainably support. Overshooting it often leads to increased mortality, decreased birth rates, or both.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because carrying capacity is determined by environmental conditions and available resources, not by the population simply demanding more support.
Correct. After overshoot, density-dependent limits such as competition and disease commonly reduce growth and can push population size back toward carrying capacity.
This choice is partially correct because overshoot can lead to a crash, but it is too absolute. Some populations decline and then fluctuate around carrying capacity rather than going extinct.
This choice is wrong because carrying capacity is specifically about the maximum sustainable population size, not body size.

questions.text:
Ecologists construct an age-structure diagram for a country and find a broad base and relatively fewer older individuals.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The age structure is not useful because only population density, never age distribution, can be used to predict future population trends.
The pattern proves that carrying capacity has already been exceeded, because any uneven age structure means the environment is overpopulated.
The population must be in decline because a broad base means death rates already exceed birth rates.
The population is likely to grow because many individuals are entering or approaching reproductive age.
questions.explain: Age-structure diagrams help predict future population trends by showing how many individuals are in pre-reproductive, reproductive, and post-reproductive groups.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because age structure is specifically useful for predicting future growth potential or decline.
This choice is partially correct because age structure can reflect environmental conditions, but it is wrong because uneven age structure alone does not prove carrying capacity has been exceeded.
This choice is wrong because a broad base usually suggests many young individuals, which more often predicts growth rather than decline.
Correct. A broad young cohort usually indicates future population growth as those individuals mature and reproduce.

questions.text:
Ecologists construct an age-structure diagram for a rabbit population and find a narrow base and a large older cohort.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The population must grow rapidly because having many older individuals proves reproduction is currently highest.
The population is likely to decline or age because relatively few young individuals are available to replace older cohorts.
The age structure is not useful because only population density, never age distribution, can be used to predict future population trends.
The pattern proves that carrying capacity has already been exceeded, because any uneven age structure means the environment is overpopulated.
questions.explain: Age-structure diagrams help predict future population trends by showing how many individuals are in pre-reproductive, reproductive, and post-reproductive groups.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because many older individuals do not by themselves predict rapid future growth. Future growth depends strongly on the size of younger cohorts that will enter reproduction.
Correct. A narrow young cohort suggests fewer future breeders and can indicate slowing growth or decline.
This choice is wrong because age structure is specifically useful for predicting future growth potential or decline.
This choice is partially correct because age structure can reflect environmental conditions, but it is wrong because uneven age structure alone does not prove carrying capacity has been exceeded.

questions.text:
Ecologists construct an age-structure diagram for a goat herd and find a strongly top-heavy age distribution.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The population is likely to decline or age because relatively few young individuals are available to replace older cohorts.
The pattern proves that carrying capacity has already been exceeded, because any uneven age structure means the environment is overpopulated.
The age structure is not useful because only population density, never age distribution, can be used to predict future population trends.
The population must grow rapidly because having many older individuals proves reproduction is currently highest.
questions.explain: Age-structure diagrams help predict future population trends by showing how many individuals are in pre-reproductive, reproductive, and post-reproductive groups.
choice_explanations: Correct. A narrow young cohort suggests fewer future breeders and can indicate slowing growth or decline.
This choice is partially correct because age structure can reflect environmental conditions, but it is wrong because uneven age structure alone does not prove carrying capacity has been exceeded.
This choice is wrong because age structure is specifically useful for predicting future growth potential or decline.
This choice is wrong because many older individuals do not by themselves predict rapid future growth. Future growth depends strongly on the size of younger cohorts that will enter reproduction.

questions.text: Which life history trait would be most expected in annual weeds in disturbed soil?
choices: Early reproduction, many small offspring, and little parental investment because these traits favor rapid population increase in unstable conditions.
Delayed reproduction, few offspring, and high parental care because these traits maximize survival when mortality is highest early in life.
No trade-offs between reproduction and survival because life history strategies do not involve resource allocation limits.
High parental investment only after carrying capacity is reached because life history traits change instantly with short-term population size.
questions.explain: Life history strategies reflect trade-offs among reproduction, growth, and survival. Species in unstable environments often reproduce early with many offspring, while species in stable environments often invest more in fewer offspring.
choice_explanations: Correct. In unstable or short-lived habitats, selection often favors rapid reproduction and many offspring with low parental investment.

This choice is partially correct because delayed reproduction and high parental care can be advantageous in some species, but it is less consistent with organisms adapted to unstable habitats that favor rapid increase.
This choice is wrong because life history strategies are built around trade-offs among growth, reproduction, and survival.
This choice is wrong because life history traits are evolved patterns, not immediate switches triggered simply by current carrying capacity.

This choice is partially correct because selection can favor high performance, but it is wrong because trade-offs prevent all life history traits from being maximized simultaneously.

This choice is wrong because long-lived species in stable environments often produce fewer offspring and invest more in each one.

questions.text:
Ecologists observe that two warbler species feed in different parts of the same tree: one near the trunk and one near the outer branches.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The species have identical realized niches, which proves competition cannot occur between them.
The species are violating the competitive exclusion principle, because competing species are never able to coexist in any form.
The species are partitioning resources, which can reduce direct competition by allowing them to use different parts of the niche space.
The species are showing commensalism, because one species changes its behavior while the other is unaffected.
questions.explain: Resource partitioning allows similar species to coexist by reducing direct competition for exactly the same limiting resource.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because using different resources or conditions means their realized niches are not identical. Competition is often reduced precisely because they differ.

This choice is partially correct because the competitive exclusion principle concerns competition for the same limiting resource, but it is wrong because coexistence is possible when species partition resources and reduce niche overlap.
Correct. Resource partitioning occurs when species use different resources, times, or microhabitats, reducing direct competition.
This choice is wrong because the observation describes reduced competition through niche differentiation, not a relationship in which one species benefits and the other is unaffected.

questions.text: Which prediction best follows from the competitive exclusion principle for two Paramecium species grown together while competing for the same bacterial food source?
choices: The species will coexist indefinitely as long as both are alive, because competition changes only population size and never species composition.
If the species continue to rely on the exact same limiting resource under the same conditions, one species is likely to outcompete the other over time.
The principle applies only to predators and prey, not to species at the same trophic level using the same resource.
The two species will always form a mutualism because shared resource use naturally drives cooperation rather than competition.
questions.explain: The competitive exclusion principle states that two species cannot occupy exactly the same niche and compete indefinitely for the same limiting resource under identical conditions.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because competition can certainly affect population size, but it is wrong because strong competition can also lead to exclusion of one species.
Correct. Competitive exclusion predicts that complete niche overlap for a limiting resource cannot be maintained indefinitely.
This choice is wrong because competitive exclusion is especially relevant to species using the same limiting resource at the same trophic level.
This choice is wrong because shared use of a limiting resource increases competition, not automatic mutualism.

questions.text:
Ecologists observe that after sea otters decline, sea urchin numbers rise and kelp cover falls.
Which explanation best fits this pattern?
choices: The pattern shows density-independent control, because predators affect prey regardless of ecological context.
Primary productivity must have increased, because every decline in a predator immediately adds energy to the ecosystem.
The result proves mutualism between predators and producers, because predator loss often harms plants indirectly.
The top predator likely helped control lower trophic levels, so removing it triggered a trophic cascade through the food web.
questions.explain: A trophic cascade occurs when a change at one trophic level, especially near the top of a food web, indirectly affects organisms at lower trophic levels.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because predation is a biotic interaction and its impact commonly depends on population densities and food-web structure.
This choice is wrong because predator removal does not automatically increase total energy entering the ecosystem. The pattern is about changing species interactions across trophic levels.
This choice is partially correct because predator removal can indirectly reduce producer abundance, but it is wrong because that indirect effect is not mutualism.
Correct. A trophic cascade occurs when changes at a higher trophic level indirectly alter organisms at lower levels.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why ecologists might classify sea stars in a rocky intertidal zone as a keystone or otherwise especially influential species?
choices: They can have a disproportionately large effect on community structure because their removal allows mussels to dominate and sharply lowers overall diversity.
They are automatically primary producers, because only producers can shape the rest of a community.
They must be the most abundant species in the ecosystem, because influence is measured only by biomass.
They cannot be replaced by any other species because ecological roles are always completely unique and nonoverlapping.
questions.explain: A keystone species has a disproportionately large effect on community structure relative to its abundance. Some highly influential species also function as ecosystem engineers or foundation species.
choice_explanations: Correct. A keystone species has an effect on community structure that is large relative to its abundance, and their removal allows mussels to dominate and sharply lowers overall diversity.
This choice is wrong because many keystone species are consumers or ecosystem engineers rather than producers.
This choice is wrong because keystone effects are defined by disproportionate influence, not by being the most abundant species.

This choice is partially correct because some influential roles are hard to replace, but it is too absolute. Ecological roles can overlap to some degree, and keystone status depends on observed community effects.

choices: They are automatically primary producers, because only producers can shape the rest of a community.
They can have a disproportionately large effect on community structure because their roots create nursery habitat for many aquatic organisms.
They must be the most abundant species in the ecosystem, because influence is measured only by biomass.
They cannot be replaced by any other species because ecological roles are always completely unique and nonoverlapping.
questions.explain: A keystone species has a disproportionately large effect on community structure relative to its abundance. Some highly influential species also function as ecosystem engineers or foundation species.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because many keystone species are consumers or ecosystem engineers rather than producers.
Correct. A keystone species has an effect on community structure that is large relative to its abundance, and their roots create nursery habitat for many aquatic organisms.
This choice is wrong because keystone effects are defined by disproportionate influence, not by being the most abundant species.

questions.text:
In a wetland plot, gross primary productivity is measured as 1200 units of energy per square meter per year, and producers use 700 of those units in respiration.
What is the net primary productivity of the plot?
questions.explain: Net primary productivity is the amount of energy stored by producers after subtracting the energy they use in respiration from gross primary productivity.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because gross primary productivity is part of the calculation, but it is wrong because GPP includes energy later used in respiration. NPP is the remainder after respiration is subtracted.
This choice is wrong because respiration alone is not net primary productivity. It is the amount of captured energy used by producers themselves.
Correct. Net primary productivity equals gross primary productivity minus respiration: 1200 - 700 = 500.
This choice is wrong because it adds respiration to gross primary productivity. NPP is calculated by subtracting respiration from GPP, not adding it.

questions.text:
A grassland food chain contains 12000 kJ of energy stored in producers.
Using the common ecological approximation for trophic transfer, about how much energy would be available to primary consumers?
questions.explain: Only a small fraction of energy, often approximated as about 10%, is transferred from one trophic level to the next.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because it assumes 90% of the energy moves to the next level. In most ecosystems, much of the energy is lost as heat and through metabolism rather than transferred.
This choice is partially correct because some later trophic transfers can make energy appear much smaller, but from producers to primary consumers the common approximation is about 10%, not 1%.
Correct. About 10% of the energy at one trophic level is commonly transferred to the next, so roughly 1200 kJ would be available to primary consumers.
This choice is wrong because the next trophic level does not receive all of the energy stored in producers.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why biomass usually decreases from lower to higher trophic levels in a forest?
choices: At each trophic transfer, much of the energy in consumed biomass is used in metabolism or lost as heat, so less biomass can be supported at higher levels.
Biomass declines only because decomposers remove matter from ecosystems rather than recycling it.
Biomass declines because matter cannot move between trophic levels, so consumers cannot incorporate what they eat.
Biomass must decline because producers are always physically smaller than consumers.
questions.explain: Biomass usually declines at higher trophic levels because only a fraction of energy and matter consumed is converted into new biomass.
choice_explanations: Correct. Higher trophic levels generally contain less biomass because inefficient energy transfer limits how much living mass can be supported.

This choice is partially correct because decomposers do process dead organic matter, but it is wrong because decomposition recycles matter. Biomass decline up the pyramid mainly reflects inefficient energy transfer.
This choice is wrong because matter absolutely does move between trophic levels through feeding. The limitation is that not all ingested energy is converted into new biomass.
This choice is wrong because producer size is not the reason biomass pyramids usually decline. The main reason is energy loss between trophic levels.

questions.text:
Ecologists detect mercury in an aquatic food web and find the highest concentrations in ospreys.
Which explanation best accounts for this pattern?
choices: The contaminant concentration is highest in top predators only because they photosynthesize more slowly than producers.
The contaminant biomagnifies because organisms at higher trophic levels consume many contaminated prey and concentrate the toxin in their tissues over time.
The contaminant must be produced by top predators themselves, because the highest concentration always identifies the original source organism.
The pattern shows ecological pyramids are reversed because predators store more matter than producers.
questions.explain: Biomagnification is the increase in toxin concentration at higher trophic levels, especially for persistent fat-soluble contaminants.

questions.text: Which nitrogen-cycle process is best described by the following statement: soil bacteria convert atmospheric N2 into ammonia?
choices: nitrogen fixation
ecological succession
carbon fixation
transpiration
questions.explain: The nitrogen cycle includes distinct processes such as nitrogen fixation, nitrification, ammonification, and denitrification.
choice_explanations: Correct. Soil bacteria convert atmospheric n2 into ammonia describes nitrogen fixation.
This choice is partially correct because succession can affect nutrient cycling over time, but it is not the specific nitrogen transformation described.
This choice is wrong because carbon fixation refers to incorporating inorganic carbon into organic molecules, not converting nitrogen among forms.
This choice is wrong because transpiration is water loss from plants, not a nitrogen-cycle process.

questions.text: Which statement best describes how the carbon cycle is affected when large areas of forest are burned?
choices: More carbon is released to the atmosphere and fewer trees remain to remove carbon dioxide by photosynthesis.
Only the nitrogen cycle is affected, because carbon movement depends on photosynthesis alone and not on respiration or combustion.
Atmospheric carbon dioxide must decrease because all ecosystem disturbance immediately traps carbon in soil.
Carbon cycling stops until producers recolonize, because decomposers do not participate in carbon transfer.
questions.explain: Carbon moves among reservoirs through photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, combustion, and long-term storage.
choice_explanations: Correct. When large areas of forest are burned, more carbon is released to the atmosphere and fewer trees remain to remove carbon dioxide by photosynthesis.
This choice is partially correct because photosynthesis is a major carbon-cycle process, but it is wrong because respiration, decomposition, and combustion are also central carbon fluxes.
This choice is wrong because many disturbances release stored carbon or reduce carbon uptake rather than trapping more carbon.
This choice is wrong because decomposers actively participate in the carbon cycle by respiring carbon back to the atmosphere or water.

questions.text: Which prediction about the water cycle is most likely when a forest is replaced by a parking lot?
choices: Only groundwater is affected, because surface processes do not influence the hydrologic cycle.
Runoff generally increases while infiltration decreases.
Transpiration is unaffected because the water cycle depends only on evaporation from oceans.
Runoff and infiltration always increase together because more moving water means more soil absorption at the same time.
questions.explain: Land-use changes can alter runoff, infiltration, groundwater recharge, evaporation, and transpiration in the water cycle.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because groundwater can be affected, but surface processes such as runoff, transpiration, and evaporation are also central parts of the water cycle.
Correct. When a forest is replaced by a parking lot, runoff generally increases while infiltration decreases.
This choice is wrong because plant transpiration is an important part of water cycling on land.
This choice is wrong because runoff and infiltration often change in opposite directions when land cover changes.

questions.text: Which statement best describes ecological succession beginning on new volcanic rock after a lava flow?
choices: It is exponential growth because any colonizing population on a new surface is undergoing only population-level change.
It is secondary succession because all disturbed habitats are classified as secondary once organisms begin to colonize them.
It is primary succession because the process begins where little or no soil is present.
It is a climax community because pioneer species immediately create a stable final state.
questions.explain: Primary succession begins on surfaces where soil is absent, such as new rock exposed by lava or glacial retreat.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because succession concerns changes in community composition over time, not just population growth of a single species.
This choice is wrong because the key distinction is soil presence. If soil is absent, the process is primary succession, not secondary succession.
Correct. Primary succession begins on surfaces lacking developed soil, so pioneer species must help create conditions for later communities.
This choice is partially correct because pioneer species do alter conditions, but they do not immediately create the final stable community.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why succession on a forest fire that burns vegetation but leaves the soil intact is classified as secondary succession?
choices: The soil remains, so recolonization can proceed using surviving organisms, seed banks, or nearby colonists.
It must be primary succession because the previous community was removed, and any removal of organisms resets all ecosystems completely.
The process concerns only abiotic change, because communities do not change after disturbance if soil is present.
No living organisms can return until lichens first weather the rock into soil, because all succession begins on bare rock.
questions.explain: Secondary succession begins after a disturbance that changes the community but leaves the soil intact.
choice_explanations: Correct. Secondary succession occurs when a disturbance changes the community but leaves the soil largely intact.
This choice is partially correct because disturbance can strongly alter a community, but it is wrong because the presence of soil means the process is secondary, not primary.
This choice is wrong because community composition often changes substantially during secondary succession even when soil remains.
This choice is wrong because not all succession begins on bare rock. When soil remains, the process is secondary succession.

questions.text: According to island biogeography theory, which prediction is most supported for a large island close to the mainland?
choices: Island biogeography applies only to literal ocean islands and cannot be used to think about habitat fragments or reserves.
Smaller and more isolated islands should always contain more species because competition is weaker there.
Species richness depends only on latitude, so island size and isolation do not matter once climate is known.
Higher immigration and lower extinction generally support more species.
questions.explain: Island biogeography predicts that larger islands and less isolated islands generally support more species because extinction rates are lower and immigration rates are higher.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because the theory was developed for islands, but it is wrong because the same ideas are often applied to habitat fragments and reserves.
This choice is wrong because smaller area and greater isolation generally reduce richness by increasing extinction and lowering immigration.
This choice is wrong because island biogeography specifically emphasizes the effects of area and isolation on extinction and immigration.
Correct. Island biogeography predicts that species richness depends strongly on immigration and extinction, so higher immigration and lower extinction generally support more species.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why higher biodiversity in a grassland can increase ecosystem stability or resilience?
choices: Biodiversity increases stability only because energy transfer between trophic levels becomes 100% efficient.
More species can provide overlapping ecological functions, so if one species declines, others may partially maintain ecosystem processes.
High biodiversity guarantees that no species will ever go extinct, so ecosystem processes become permanently fixed.
Only the number of species matters, because species identity and interactions never influence ecosystem function.
questions.explain: Higher biodiversity can increase ecosystem resilience when species differ in responses to disturbance or provide overlapping ecological roles.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because biodiversity does not make trophic transfer perfectly efficient. Energy is still lost at each trophic level.
Correct. Greater biodiversity can buffer ecosystems because multiple species may contribute to similar functions, increasing resilience to disturbance.
This choice is wrong because biodiversity does not guarantee zero extinction or perfectly fixed processes.
This choice is partially correct because species richness can matter, but it is wrong because species identity, evenness, and interactions also affect ecosystem function.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a nonnative mussel spreads rapidly in a lake and outcompetes native filter feeders can strongly alter a community?
choices: Invasive species affect only abiotic conditions and do not change species interactions such as competition or predation.
Any nonnative species is automatically beneficial because it increases species richness and therefore must increase stability.
Invasive species can spread quickly and disrupt native populations because they may lack natural enemies, exploit resources efficiently, or alter habitat conditions.
The species cannot become established unless it immediately becomes the dominant primary producer.
questions.explain: Invasive species are nonnative species that spread and cause ecological or economic harm, often by altering species interactions or habitat conditions.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because invasive species often alter competition, predation, herbivory, disease, and mutualisms in addition to abiotic conditions.
This choice is partially correct because adding species can raise local richness numerically, but it is wrong because invasive species can still reduce native diversity and destabilize ecosystems.
Correct. Many invasive species become influential because they spread rapidly and change competition, predation, or habitat structure.
This choice is wrong because invasive species can belong to many trophic levels and do not need to be primary producers.

questions.text: Which sequence best describes what commonly happens when fertilizer runoff enters a lake?
choices: Primary productivity always decreases immediately because extra nutrients prevent photosynthesis in producers.
Algal growth often increases first, and later decomposition of the added biomass can lower dissolved oxygen and stress aquatic organisms.
Dissolved oxygen rises permanently because decomposers release oxygen when they break down algae.
The only major effect is warmer water temperature, because nutrients do not affect community structure or respiration.
questions.explain: Eutrophication is nutrient enrichment that often causes algal blooms and later oxygen depletion when excess biomass decomposes.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because extra nutrients usually stimulate producer growth at first rather than suppressing it immediately.
Correct. Eutrophication commonly begins with nutrient enrichment and algal blooms, followed by oxygen depletion as decomposers break down excess organic matter.
This choice is wrong because decomposers consume oxygen during aerobic respiration rather than releasing it.
This choice is partially correct because eutrophication can interact with temperature, but it is wrong because nutrients strongly affect productivity, decomposition, and oxygen availability.

questions.text: Which sequence best describes what commonly happens when agricultural drainage flows into a shallow bay?

questions.text: Which statement best explains why ocean acidification can be especially harmful to corals?
choices: Acidification increases shell growth because acids provide the calcium needed to make harder shells.
The effect occurs only in freshwater systems because oceans are too large for carbon dioxide to influence chemistry.
When more carbon dioxide dissolves in seawater, pH falls and carbonate availability declines, making calcium carbonate structures harder to build or maintain.
Ocean acidification harms them only because dissolved oxygen always falls to zero whenever pH declines slightly.
questions.explain: Ocean acidification results largely from the absorption of atmospheric carbon dioxide by seawater, which lowers pH and can reduce calcification.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because acidification generally makes calcification more difficult; it does not supply usable calcium in a way that strengthens shells.
This choice is partially correct because freshwater chemistry can also change, but it is wrong because atmospheric carbon dioxide does alter ocean chemistry on a global scale.
Correct. Ocean acidification lowers pH and reduces carbonate ion availability, which can impair calcification in many marine organisms.
This choice is wrong because lower pH does not automatically drive dissolved oxygen to zero. The specific concern here is reduced carbonate availability for calcification.

questions.text: Which consequence is most likely when a forest is divided into many small patches by roads and housing?
choices: Edge effects often increase, and populations in the isolated patches may experience reduced movement, altered microclimate, and higher local extinction risk.
Species richness must rise in every patch because smaller areas always support more species than larger continuous habitats.
Only abiotic conditions change, because fragmentation does not affect dispersal, predation, or community composition.
Gene flow usually increases because organisms prefer to move across developed land between habitat fragments.
questions.explain: Habitat fragmentation can isolate populations, increase edge effects, reduce gene flow, and raise the risk of local extinction.
choice_explanations: Correct. Fragmentation often increases edge habitat and isolates populations, which can change conditions and reduce persistence.
This choice is wrong because smaller isolated patches typically support fewer species than larger connected habitats.
This choice is partially correct because abiotic edge conditions can change, but it is wrong because fragmentation also affects dispersal and species interactions.
This choice is wrong because fragmentation often decreases movement and gene flow among patches.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a fishery sets harvest limits below the population’s long-term replacement rate is considered a more sustainable practice?
choices: It aims to maintain ecosystem function or resource availability over time rather than maximizing short-term extraction only.
It guarantees that ecosystems will never change again, because sustainability means preventing all natural disturbance.
It eliminates all human impact from the system, which is required before any practice can be called sustainable.
It is sustainable only if it increases short-term yield immediately, because long-term resilience matters less than annual output.
questions.explain: Sustainable practices are designed to maintain resources and ecosystem services over the long term while reducing environmental degradation.
choice_explanations: Correct. Sustainable practices seek to preserve long-term ecosystem services or resource stocks while reducing damage.
This choice is wrong because ecosystems remain dynamic. Sustainability does not mean freezing them in one state forever.
This choice is wrong because sustainability does not require zero human impact. It requires using resources in ways that can be maintained over time.
This choice is partially correct because yield can matter, but it is wrong because sustainability emphasizes long-term persistence and resilience, not just immediate output.

questions.text:
Ecologists estimate the size of a turtles population using mark–recapture.
In the first sample, they capture, mark, and release 40 individuals. Later they capture 100 individuals, and 20 of those are marked.
Based on these data, what is the best estimate of the total population size?
questions.explain: Mark–recapture estimates population size by relating the proportion of marked individuals in the second sample to the proportion marked in the entire population.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because it multiplies the first and second samples but does not divide by the proportion of marked individuals recaptured.
Correct. The mark–recapture estimate is (M × C) / R = (40 × 100) / 20 = 200.
This choice is partially correct because recaptures matter, but subtracting recaptures from the second sample does not estimate total population size.
This choice is wrong because it adds and subtracts sample counts instead of using the mark–recapture relationship (M × C) / R.

questions.text:
Students place quadrats at four positions along a transect in a salt marsh. Species A is recorded at [2, 5, 9, 14] individuals per quadrat from position 1 to 4, while Species B is recorded at [18, 12, 6, 1] individuals per quadrat. The abiotic gradient is that salinity increases from inland to shoreline.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The gradient cannot affect the species because both are found in the same ecosystem, so they must share the same realized niche exactly.
The two species likely differ in tolerance or niche use along the abiotic gradient, with Species A favored at one end and Species B at the other.
The pattern proves competitive exclusion has already eliminated one species from the habitat entirely.
The data prove the two species are mutualists, because one species is common whenever the other species is rare.
questions.explain: Transect data can reveal how species abundance changes along abiotic gradients, helping ecologists infer differences in niche use or environmental tolerance.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because species within one ecosystem can still occupy different microhabitats or have different abiotic tolerances.
Correct. Opposite abundance patterns along the transect support the conclusion that the species differ in how they perform across the gradient.

This choice is partially correct because competition could contribute to zonation, but it is wrong because both species are still present along the transect and the pattern could also reflect abiotic differences.
This choice is wrong because opposite abundance patterns do not demonstrate mutualism. They more directly suggest different environmental tolerances or habitat preferences.

In a salt marsh, ecologists set up paired plots. In open plots, crab can enter normally. In exclusion plots, crab are kept out. After two months, exclusion plots contain more snail and less marsh grass than open plots.
Which explanation is best supported?
choices: Crab normally reduce snail numbers, so excluding the predator increases grazing pressure and indirectly lowers marsh grass.
Crab are mutualists of marsh grass, because producers always grow best when predators are absent.
Snail must be primary producers, because only producers can directly reduce marsh grass abundance.
The result proves abiotic conditions changed between the plots, because predator removal cannot affect lower trophic levels indirectly.
questions.explain: Predator-exclusion experiments can reveal top-down control and trophic cascades when removing predators changes herbivores and producers indirectly.
choice_explanations: Correct. The exclusion treatment removed top-down control by crab, allowing snail to increase and indirectly reducing marsh grass.
This choice is wrong because the data show producers declined when predators were absent. That pattern supports indirect protection of the producer by the predator, not a direct mutualism.
This choice is wrong because herbivores or grazers can reduce producer abundance by consuming plant or algal tissue even though they are not producers themselves.
This choice is partially correct because abiotic differences should always be considered, but it is wrong because predator exclusion is a classic way to test top-down effects on lower trophic levels.

questions.text:
Researchers add nitrate to experimental enclosures in a pond while leaving control enclosures unaltered. One week later, chlorophyll concentration rises from 5 to 26 µg/L in the nutrient-added enclosures, and dissolved oxygen just before dawn falls from 7.5 to 3.4 mg/L.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The added nutrient stimulated algal growth, and later respiration and decomposition likely contributed to lower dissolved oxygen.
The added nutrient directly poisoned algae, because chlorophyll concentrations always rise when producers die.
Dissolved oxygen fell because photosynthesis stopped completely in all producers as soon as nutrients were added.
The data prove the water warmed dramatically, because nutrient enrichment cannot affect oxygen through biological processes.
questions.explain: Nutrient-addition experiments often show eutrophication: increased algal biomass followed by lower dissolved oxygen, especially after respiration and decomposition intensify.
choice_explanations: Correct. Rising chlorophyll indicates algal growth, and the drop in predawn dissolved oxygen is consistent with eutrophication-driven respiration and decomposition.
This choice is wrong because rising chlorophyll indicates more algal biomass, not direct algal poisoning.
This choice is wrong because nutrient addition commonly increases, not immediately stops, producer growth. Lower predawn oxygen is more consistent with elevated respiration and decomposition.

This choice is partially correct because temperature can influence oxygen, but it is wrong because nutrient enrichment commonly changes oxygen through biological processes such as algal blooms and decomposition.

questions.text:
Students use the light-and-dark bottle method in a lake. Initial dissolved oxygen is 10.2 mg/L. After incubation, the light bottle is 14.6 mg/L and the dark bottle is 7.4 mg/L.
Which statement is correct?
choices: Net primary productivity is zero because the light and dark bottles both contain water from the same ecosystem.
Net primary productivity is 4.4 mg/L and gross primary productivity is 7.2 mg/L over the incubation period.
Net primary productivity is 2.8 mg/L and gross primary productivity is 4.4 mg/L, because the dark bottle shows photosynthesis directly.
Gross primary productivity is 7.4 mg/L because the dark bottle contains only the oxygen that producers made before incubation.
questions.explain: In the light bottle, oxygen change reflects net primary productivity. The dark bottle shows respiration, which allows gross primary productivity to be calculated.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because both bottles begin from the same ecosystem water, but it is wrong because differences in oxygen change between them are exactly what allow productivity to be estimated.
Correct. NPP = light - initial = 4.4; respiration = initial - dark = 2.8; GPP = NPP + respiration = 7.2.
This choice is wrong because the dark bottle does not show photosynthesis directly; it shows oxygen loss due to respiration in the absence of light.
This choice is wrong because gross primary productivity is a rate calculated from oxygen changes, not simply the final oxygen concentration in the dark bottle.

Net primary productivity is 3.3 mg/L and gross primary productivity is 3.3 mg/L, because the dark bottle shows photosynthesis directly.
Net primary productivity is 3.3 mg/L and gross primary productivity is 6.6 mg/L over the incubation period.
Gross primary productivity is 7.2 mg/L because the dark bottle contains only the oxygen that producers made before incubation.
questions.explain: In the light bottle, oxygen change reflects net primary productivity. The dark bottle shows respiration, which allows gross primary productivity to be calculated.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because both bottles begin from the same ecosystem water, but it is wrong because differences in oxygen change between them are exactly what allow productivity to be estimated.
This choice is wrong because the dark bottle does not show photosynthesis directly; it shows oxygen loss due to respiration in the absence of light.
Correct. NPP = light - initial = 3.3; respiration = initial - dark = 3.3; GPP = NPP + respiration = 6.6.
This choice is wrong because gross primary productivity is a rate calculated from oxygen changes, not simply the final oxygen concentration in the dark bottle.

questions.text:
Ecologists place identical leaf-litter bags in two habitats. After six weeks, bags in the cool dry retain 72% of their original mass, whereas bags in the warm wet retain 31%.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Decomposition was faster in the warm wet, likely because conditions there favored decomposer activity more than in the cool dry.
Decomposition was faster in the cool dry, because higher remaining mass always means more energy transfer to decomposers.
The experiment cannot compare decomposition because litter bags measure only photosynthesis, not breakdown of organic matter.
The results prove decomposers are absent from the habitat with higher remaining mass, because any slower decomposition means zero decomposer activity.
questions.explain: Lower remaining mass in litter bags indicates faster decomposition, which is often promoted by warmer, wetter, or otherwise more favorable conditions for decomposers.
choice_explanations: Correct. Lower remaining mass means faster decomposition, so the warm wet supported greater decomposer activity or conditions that increased breakdown.
This choice is wrong because higher remaining mass means less decomposition occurred, not more.
This choice is wrong because litter-bag mass loss is a standard way to measure decomposition of organic matter.
This choice is partially correct because slower mass loss can indicate less decomposer activity, but it is wrong because slower decomposition does not prove decomposers are absent.

questions.text:
In a grassland, ecologists add nitrogen fertilizer to some plots and leave other plots as controls. After one growing season, control plots average 12 plant species, while fertilized plots average 7 species and are dominated by one fast-growing grass.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Species richness declined because the nutrient eliminated photosynthesis in the dominant grass.
Nutrient addition likely reduced species richness by allowing a few strong competitors to dominate and exclude other species.
The data prove nutrient enrichment can never increase plant biomass, because communities with fewer species must contain less total plant growth.
The fertilizer must have killed most plants directly, because lower richness always means lower productivity.
questions.explain: Nutrient enrichment can reduce plant diversity if a few fast-growing species become dominant and competitively exclude others.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because dominance by a fast-growing grass indicates that species benefited from the added nutrient rather than losing photosynthesis.
Correct. Fertilization can increase growth of already competitive species, reducing richness when those species monopolize light or other resources.
This choice is partially correct because richness and biomass can sometimes covary, but it is wrong because lower richness can still occur alongside higher biomass if one species becomes dominant.
This choice is wrong because the prompt states one fast-growing grass dominates, which is more consistent with competitive exclusion than direct poisoning.

Biologists survey bird species on an island classified as small near relative to a mainland source and record 12 species.
Which prediction is most consistent with island biogeography theory?
choices: Island size matters only for plant species, so bird richness should be unrelated to area or isolation.
The island should have fewer species than a similar-sized island that is closer to the mainland, because greater isolation lowers immigration.
A distant island should always have more species than a near island because isolation prevents competition from arriving.
Species richness should depend only on current climate, because immigration and extinction are not part of island biogeography.
questions.explain: Island biogeography predicts that larger and less isolated islands generally support more species because extinction is lower and immigration is higher.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because island biogeography applies broadly to many taxa, including birds.
Correct. The prediction follows island biogeography: species richness depends strongly on island size and isolation.
This choice is partially correct because isolation can reduce immigration of competitors, but it is wrong because isolation usually lowers overall species richness by limiting immigration.
This choice is wrong because immigration and extinction are central to island biogeography theory.

questions.text:
Conservation biologists compare habitat patches connected by corridors with similar isolated patches. Over a season, they record 47 movements of tagged mice between connected patches but only 20 movements between isolated patches.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Corridors always increase carrying capacity within each patch, so movement data are not relevant to conservation.
Habitat corridors can increase movement among patches, which may reduce isolation and help maintain gene flow or recolonization.
The data prove corridors eliminate all edge effects, because any increase in movement removes fragment boundaries.
Movement is higher in connected patches only because organisms are forced to leave lower-quality habitat, so corridors cannot aid conservation.
questions.explain: Corridors are used in conservation to connect habitat patches and can increase movement, recolonization, and gene flow among otherwise isolated populations.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because the measured effect here is movement between patches, not automatic change in carrying capacity within each patch.
Correct. Higher movement between connected patches supports the idea that corridors reduce isolation.
This choice is wrong because corridors may improve connectivity without eliminating edge effects or all fragmentation problems.
This choice is partially correct because habitat quality can influence movement, but it is wrong because the observed increase in movement is still consistent with corridors improving connectivity.

questions.text:
Managers remove zebra mussels from treatment plots while leaving nearby control plots unchanged. Over the next season, treatment plots show higher native mussels abundance or improved water clarity compared with controls.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The invasive species influenced only species richness and could not have affected water clarity or ecosystem processes.
The invasive species must have been a keystone mutualist, because removing any abundant species should improve all ecosystem responses.
The change proves abiotic conditions differed before the experiment, because species removal experiments cannot reveal ecological effects.
The invasive species was likely suppressing native populations or ecosystem function, so its removal allowed recovery in the treatment plots.
questions.explain: If removing an invasive species improves native abundance or ecosystem function, the data support a negative ecological effect of the invasive species.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because invasives can affect richness, but it is wrong because they can also alter ecosystem processes and habitat conditions.
This choice is wrong because the observed improvement after removal suggests the invasive species was harmful rather than beneficial in this context.
This choice is wrong because manipulative removal experiments are specifically designed to test ecological effects, even though other variables should still be controlled.
Correct. Improvement after removal supports the conclusion that zebra mussels had negative ecological effects on the system.

questions.text:
Managers remove Asian carp from treatment plots while leaving nearby control plots unchanged. Over the next season, treatment plots show higher native planktivorous fish abundance or improved zooplankton abundance compared with controls.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The invasive species was likely suppressing native populations or ecosystem function, so its removal allowed recovery in the treatment plots.
The invasive species must have been a keystone mutualist, because removing any abundant species should improve all ecosystem responses.
The change proves abiotic conditions differed before the experiment, because species removal experiments cannot reveal ecological effects.
The invasive species influenced only species richness and could not have affected zooplankton abundance or ecosystem processes.
questions.explain: If removing an invasive species improves native abundance or ecosystem function, the data support a negative ecological effect of the invasive species.
choice_explanations: Correct. Improvement after removal supports the conclusion that Asian carp had negative ecological effects on the system.
This choice is wrong because the observed improvement after removal suggests the invasive species was harmful rather than beneficial in this context.
This choice is wrong because manipulative removal experiments are specifically designed to test ecological effects, even though other variables should still be controlled.
This choice is partially correct because invasives can affect richness, but it is wrong because they can also alter ecosystem processes and habitat conditions.

questions.text:
In park A, wolves are reintroduced. Three years later, average elk browsing intensity drops from 58% of shoots browsed to 39%, while willow stem density rises from 15 to 46 stems per plot.
Which explanation is best supported?
choices: Willow increased because wolves directly fertilized the plants through mutualism, independent of elk behavior.
Elk browsing fell because producers always outcompete herbivores when predators are present.
Wolves likely reduced herbivory pressure directly or indirectly, allowing willow populations to recover through a trophic cascade.
The data prove wolves increased primary productivity in all parts of the ecosystem equally.
questions.explain: Predator reintroduction can trigger trophic cascades when reduced herbivory allows producer populations to recover.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because the main pattern shown is a change in herbivory pressure, not a direct mutualism between wolves and willows.
This choice is wrong because producers do not competitively exclude herbivores; the effect here is better explained by predation risk or reduced herbivore density.
Correct. Lower browsing and higher willow density are consistent with top-down effects following predator reintroduction.

This choice is partially correct because plant biomass can increase locally after herbivory declines, but it is too broad. The data specifically support reduced elk browsing and willow recovery, not equal change across the entire ecosystem.

questions.text:
Ecologists remove or greatly reduce sea star from experimental areas of a rocky intertidal. Afterward, they observe major changes in mussels and a decline in other invertebrate species relative to control areas.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Sea star must be the most abundant species, because only the most abundant species can alter communities when removed.
Sea star affects only abiotic variables and cannot influence species composition indirectly.
The results show only random drift in species composition, because manipulative experiments cannot reveal ecological roles.
Sea star likely functions as a keystone or strongly structuring species in this community.
questions.explain: If removing one species causes major community changes, ecologists may infer that the species has a disproportionately important ecological role.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because keystone or structuring effects depend on disproportionate influence, not simply abundance.
This choice is partially correct because some structuring species do alter abiotic conditions, but it is wrong because those changes can strongly influence species composition indirectly.
This choice is wrong because manipulative removal experiments are a standard way to test ecological importance.
Correct. Large community changes following reduction of sea star support the inference that it has a disproportionately strong ecological role.

questions.text:
Marine biologists rear oysters in seawater at present-day pH and in seawater with lower pH caused by added dissolved CO2. Average calcium carbonate mass gained per individual is 1.81 mg in the control and 1.5 mg in the lower-pH treatment.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The lower-pH treatment increased calcification because acidic water provides more solid carbonate for shell building.
The result proves the organisms died immediately in the lower-pH treatment, because any reduction in shell mass gain means zero survival.
Lower pH likely reduced calcification, consistent with ocean acidification making calcium carbonate structures harder to build.
The data show carbon dioxide affects only temperature and therefore cannot alter seawater chemistry.
questions.explain: Experiments comparing present-day and lower-pH seawater can test whether ocean acidification reduces calcification in marine organisms.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because lower pH generally reduces carbonate availability for calcification rather than increasing it.
This choice is wrong because reduced calcification does not by itself prove immediate death.
Correct. Reduced shell or skeletal mass gain under lower pH is consistent with ocean acidification impairing calcification.
This choice is partially correct because atmospheric CO2 is linked to climate warming, but it is wrong because dissolved CO2 also directly alters seawater chemistry.

The lower-pH treatment increased calcification because acidic water provides more solid carbonate for shell building.
Lower pH likely reduced calcification, consistent with ocean acidification making calcium carbonate structures harder to build.
The data show carbon dioxide affects only temperature and therefore cannot alter seawater chemistry.
questions.explain: Experiments comparing present-day and lower-pH seawater can test whether ocean acidification reduces calcification in marine organisms.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because reduced calcification does not by itself prove immediate death.
This choice is wrong because lower pH generally reduces carbonate availability for calcification rather than increasing it.
Correct. Reduced shell or skeletal mass gain under lower pH is consistent with ocean acidification impairing calcification.
This choice is partially correct because atmospheric CO2 is linked to climate warming, but it is wrong because dissolved CO2 also directly alters seawater chemistry.

questions.text:
Coral fragments from reef site 1 are kept for four weeks at a typical summer temperature or at a temperature 2.5°C higher. Bleaching occurs in 11% of colonies at the typical temperature and 69% at the warmer temperature.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Higher temperature increased bleaching, consistent with heat stress disrupting the coral–algal symbiosis.
Bleaching increased because corals photosynthesize more efficiently at higher temperature and therefore expel symbionts to avoid overgrowth.
The pattern shows temperature affects only coral color and not ecological function, because symbiont loss does not influence energy balance.
The data prove nutrient pollution was the only cause of bleaching, because temperature changes cannot alter coral symbiosis.
questions.explain: Coral bleaching experiments test whether elevated temperature disrupts the mutualism between corals and their algal symbionts.
choice_explanations: Correct. Increased bleaching under warmer conditions supports the conclusion that heat stress destabilizes the mutualism between corals and their photosynthetic symbionts.
This choice is wrong because bleaching reflects stress and breakdown of the mutualism, not improved photosynthetic efficiency benefiting the coral.
This choice is partially correct because bleaching is visible as color loss, but it is wrong because loss of symbionts strongly affects coral energy acquisition and survival.
This choice is wrong because the experiment specifically manipulated temperature and observed more bleaching in the warmer treatment.

questions.text:
Ecologists compare habitat edges with interior forest. They find that nest predation, desiccation stress, or mortality affecting songbirds averages 41% at edges but only 25% in interior plots.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The difference proves habitat area is irrelevant, because only the border itself determines whether species survive.
Edges must contain more total species, so any species found there should perform better automatically.
Edge effects are altering conditions near habitat boundaries, making edges less suitable for this forest-associated species than interior habitat.
The data show fragmentation affects only abiotic variables and not ecological interactions such as predation.
questions.explain: Edge-effect studies compare boundaries and interior habitat to test how fragmentation changes conditions and species performance.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because habitat area and edge-to-interior ratio both matter; the result does not show area is irrelevant.
This choice is wrong because even if some edge species increase, interior-associated species can perform worse at edges.
Correct. Higher mortality or stress at habitat edges supports the conclusion that edge conditions differ biologically or physically from interiors.
This choice is partially correct because edge effects often include abiotic changes, but it is wrong because predation and other biotic interactions can also change at edges.

questions.text:
After similar rain events at a suburban block, researchers compare a vegetated plot with a paved plot. The vegetated plot shows infiltration of 3.7 cm/hr and runoff of 0.7 L/min, whereas the paved plot shows infiltration of 0.9 cm/hr and runoff of 4.0 L/min.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Vegetation mainly changes the nitrogen cycle, so the water cycle measurements are unrelated to land cover.
Impervious surfaces reduce infiltration and increase runoff, which can alter the local water cycle and increase flooding risk.
Pavement increases groundwater recharge because water moves more quickly across it into drains.
Runoff rose only because the paved plot must have received more rain, because surface type cannot affect runoff from equal storms.
questions.explain: Comparing paved and vegetated surfaces can show how land cover affects infiltration and runoff in the hydrologic cycle.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because vegetation strongly affects infiltration, runoff, and evapotranspiration in the water cycle.
Correct. Lower infiltration and higher runoff on pavement are exactly the hydrologic effects expected from impervious surfaces.
This choice is wrong because rapid surface flow to drains is not the same as infiltration into soil and recharge of groundwater.
This choice is partially correct because rainfall amount matters, but it is wrong because surface type clearly affects runoff even under equal storms.

After similar rain events at a industrial area, researchers compare a vegetated plot with a paved plot. The vegetated plot shows infiltration of 3.7 cm/hr and runoff of 0.9 L/min, whereas the paved plot shows infiltration of 2.3 cm/hr and runoff of 3.9 L/min.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Runoff rose only because the paved plot must have received more rain, because surface type cannot affect runoff from equal storms.
Vegetation mainly changes the nitrogen cycle, so the water cycle measurements are unrelated to land cover.
Pavement increases groundwater recharge because water moves more quickly across it into drains.
Impervious surfaces reduce infiltration and increase runoff, which can alter the local water cycle and increase flooding risk.
questions.explain: Comparing paved and vegetated surfaces can show how land cover affects infiltration and runoff in the hydrologic cycle.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because rainfall amount matters, but it is wrong because surface type clearly affects runoff even under equal storms.

This choice is wrong because rapid surface flow to drains is not the same as infiltration into soil and recharge of groundwater.
Correct. Lower infiltration and higher runoff on pavement are exactly the hydrologic effects expected from impervious surfaces.

questions.text:
Managers restore a wetland along a stream reach. Water entering the wetland averages 7.6 mg/L nitrate, while water leaving averages 3.6 mg/L nitrate.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The difference proves the carbon cycle stopped in the wetland, because nitrogen removal and carbon cycling cannot occur together.
The wetland must be adding nitrate because wetlands always increase decomposition and therefore can only release nutrients.
The wetland is likely reducing nitrate through uptake and microbial processes such as denitrification, improving downstream water quality.
The decline in nitrate shows all aquatic productivity downstream must decrease permanently.
questions.explain: Wetland restoration can improve water quality by slowing water, promoting uptake, and supporting microbial transformations such as denitrification.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because multiple biogeochemical cycles operate simultaneously in wetlands.
This choice is wrong because wetlands can both store and transform nutrients; the measured drop in nitrate supports net removal here.
Correct. Lower nitrate leaving the wetland suggests the system is removing or transforming nitrogen before it moves downstream.

This choice is partially correct because nutrient availability can affect productivity, but it is too absolute. The result directly supports nitrate removal, not a permanent shutdown of downstream productivity.

questions.text:
Researchers measure mercury concentration in a lake food web. Phytoplankton average 0.08 ppm, zooplankton 0.63 ppm, small fish 2.51 ppm, and fish-eating birds 10.03 ppm.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The contaminant must be produced only by birds, because the highest concentration always identifies the organism that made the chemical.
The result proves the toxin is harmless to lower trophic levels, because they contain lower concentrations than birds.
The contaminant biomagnifies through the food web, because concentration increases at higher trophic levels.
The pattern shows energy transfer is increasing with trophic level, because higher toxin concentration means more usable energy is present.
questions.explain: Biomagnification is supported when persistent contaminants increase in concentration at successive trophic levels.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because concentration can increase through trophic transfer even when the toxin originated at lower levels or outside the ecosystem.
This choice is partially correct because lower trophic levels may contain less toxin, but it is wrong because lower concentration does not mean harmless.
Correct. The rising concentrations from producers to top consumers are consistent with biomagnification.
This choice is wrong because toxin concentration is not a measure of usable energy transfer.

questions.text:
After a wildfire that leaves soil intact, ecologists monitor plot 1. In year 1, the site is dominated by annual grasses and species richness is very low. By year 5, shrubs and perennial grasses are common and richness is moderate. By year 15, young trees appear and richness is high.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The pattern proves all species are mutually dependent on one another because richness increases over time.
The changes represent only seasonal variation, because succession cannot occur over periods as short as 15 years.
The site is undergoing primary succession because any fire removes all biological legacy and all soil nutrients.
The site is undergoing secondary succession, with community composition changing over time after disturbance because soil remained in place.
questions.explain: Post-fire monitoring of community change can reveal secondary succession when soil remains and species composition shifts over time.
choice_explanations: This choice is wrong because increasing richness does not by itself demonstrate mutual dependence among all species.
This choice is partially correct because seasonal variation affects communities, but it is wrong because succession can certainly proceed over years to decades.
This choice is wrong because the prompt states soil remains intact, which indicates secondary rather than primary succession.
Correct. The shift from early colonizers to later species after a soil-preserving disturbance is a classic pattern of secondary succession.

questions.text:
After a wildfire that leaves soil intact, ecologists monitor plot 2. In year 1, the site is dominated by annual grasses and species richness is very low. By year 5, shrubs and perennial grasses are common and richness is moderate. By year 15, young trees appear and richness is high.
Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The pattern proves all species are mutually dependent on one another because richness increases over time.
The site is undergoing primary succession because any fire removes all biological legacy and all soil nutrients.
The site is undergoing secondary succession, with community composition changing over time after disturbance because soil remained in place.
The changes represent only seasonal variation, because succession cannot occur over periods as short as 15 years.
questions.explain: Post-fire monitoring of community change can reveal secondary succession when soil remains and species composition shifts over time.

questions.text:
In a field experiment on tomato plants, some flowers are left open to pollinators and others are bagged to exclude them. Open flowers set fruit 70% of the time, whereas bagged flowers set fruit only 25% of the time.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Pollinator visitation increases reproductive success in this plant, indicating the plant depends at least partly on animal-mediated pollination.
Bagging increased reproduction by protecting flowers from herbivores, so pollinators likely reduce fitness.
The result proves the plant cannot photosynthesize without pollinators.
The data show pollinators and plants are in competition because both use floral resources.
questions.explain: Pollinator-exclusion experiments can test whether plant reproduction depends on pollinator visitation.
choice_explanations: Correct. Lower fruit set when pollinators are excluded supports the inference that pollinators contribute to reproduction.
This choice is wrong because the measured fruit set is lower, not higher, in the bagged treatment.
This choice is wrong because pollination affects reproduction, not the plant's basic ability to photosynthesize.

This choice is partially correct because pollinators do use floral resources, but it is wrong because the increased fruit set indicates a mutualistic benefit to the plant rather than competition for the same limiting resource.

questions.text:
Researchers grow corn in low-phosphorus soil. One treatment is inoculated with mycorrhizal fungi and the other is not. After six weeks, inoculated plants average 19.8 g biomass, while uninoculated plants average 16.0 g.
Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The fungi improved plant growth only by increasing light availability around the leaves.
The result proves phosphorus is not limiting in this soil, because fungal inoculation changed growth.
The fungi are parasitic because any organism living on or near a plant root must reduce plant growth.
The fungal association likely improved nutrient uptake, supporting a mutualistic relationship between the plant and the mycorrhizae.
questions.explain: Mycorrhizal experiments often test whether fungal symbionts improve plant nutrient uptake and growth, especially in nutrient-poor soils.
choice_explanations: This choice is partially correct because many factors can affect growth, but it is wrong because the manipulated factor was a root symbiont known to affect nutrient uptake, not light around leaves.
This choice is wrong because the stronger performance with mycorrhizae in low-phosphorus soil is exactly what would be expected if phosphorus is limiting.
This choice is wrong because the inoculated plants grew more, not less, which supports a benefit rather than harm to the plant.
Correct. Greater plant growth in inoculated low-phosphorus soil is consistent with the fungi helping the plant acquire nutrients.
